Turn 25 ‚ÄĒ coefficient ghost audit: group-likeness alone is not enough I re-read only the three attachments from this message. The problem remains the over-G isomorphism question for the two quotients őďS,őďH, where the statement defines F=P‚čäG, the relators rS,rH, and asks whether there is a continuous isomorphism őďS‚ÜíőďH commuting with the canonical projections to G.The attached history‚Äôs current gap is specifically the coefficient lift: the residual Ef,mod2,s=1 tower is closed, and the coefficient-and-degree compactness framework is available, but finite coefficient nonemptiness is still not proved.

The main conclusion of this turn is corrective:

Turn¬†24‚Äôs¬†proposed¬†‚Äúprove¬†ghost¬†compatibility¬†from¬†group-likeness‚ÄĚ¬†route¬†is¬†too¬†weak.It is not false in spirit: coefficient lifting really is a ghost/Bockstein problem. But group-likeness alone does not force the required ghost compatibility. The remaining coefficient gap is therefore sharper than Turn 24‚Äôs final sentence suggested.

Recall the local coefficient notation from Turn 24:

R:=Efőõ‚ČÉZ2 [[t]],t=s‚ąí1,m=(2,t).

After the Turn 1 H1 -change, the dangerous Ef-linear row is(s2+1,¬†s‚ąí1)=(2+2t+t2,¬†t).So the two first coefficient directions are indeed the 2-direction and the t-direction: over R/(4,t) one sees (2,0), and over R/(2,t2) one sees (0,t). This is exactly the ghost split identified in Turn 24.But the next inference needs care.

For an actual correction K=1+W+‚čĮ, one has the beginningK2=1+2W+W[2]+‚čĮ.Thus an actual square correction couples the coefficient-2 term 2W with the residual square W[2]. This explains why the square-zero Hensel route from Turn 22 failed: in a square-zero tangent extension, the W[2]part disappears, even though it is exactly what kills the even residual square quotient. Turn 23 already rejected that square-zero route for this reason.

However, the converse is not automatic:group-like¬†errorÓÄ† ‚áíghost¬†pair¬†of¬†the¬†form¬†(2W,W[2]).Here is the clean obstruction to the proposed shortcut. Work overA2=R/(4,t)‚ČÖZ/4.Let W be a primitive homogeneous element. Then, modulo 4,1+2W is group-like to the relevant order, because őĒ(1+2W)=1‚äó1+2(W‚äó1+1‚äóW),while (1+2W)‚äó(1+2W)=1‚äó1+2(W‚äó1+1‚äóW)+4W‚äóW‚Č°őĒ(1+2W)(mod4).So group-likeness allows a coefficient-2 ghost 2W with no forced visible W[2]component at the doubled degree. By contrast, an actual square correction K2 produces both 2W and W[2]. Therefore the identity K2=1+2W+W[2]+‚čĮ describes the image of actual corrections, not every group-like coefficient error.That is the precise flaw in trying to prove Turn 24‚Äôs ghost compatibility lemma ‚Äúdirectly from group-likeness.‚ÄĚ The lemma must be an accessibility statement for the actual lower-plus-high Nielsen torsor, not merely a Hopf-algebra group-likeness statement.The correct coefficient invariant to track is a ghost defect.For the 2-direction, suppose at a given root degree m the coefficient-level error has the schematic form 1+2U+ő©2m+‚čĮ,where

U‚ąąLm,[ő©2m]‚ąąQ2m:=L2m/im‚ąā2m‚ąí1‚ČÖLm[2].A root correction with initial form W changes the two ghosts by (U,[ő©2m])‚üľ(U+W,[ő©2m]+W[2]).If we choose W=U to remove the lower coefficient-2 term, the remaining square class becomes[ő©2m]+U[2].Thus the real question is not whether every square class is hit by W‚Ü¶W[2]in isolation. The real question is whether the defectőī2 (U,ő©):=[ő©2m]+U[2]lies in the square-moving image of corrections that preserve the already-killed lower coefficient ghosts.

Equivalently, define the lower ghost mapgm(2):Tm‚ÜíLm from all degree-m correction variables in the A2 -problem to the coefficient-2 tail. Then the relevant square-moving map isőļm(2):kergm(2)‚ü∂Q2m‚ČÖLm[2].

The A2 -coefficient obstruction vanishes at root degree m only ifőī2 (U,ő©)‚ąąimőļm(2).This is the coefficient analogue of what happened at residual degree 16: the high torsor alone missed one square direction, and only after including the lower Y0‚Ü¶Y0Y4 base deformation did the obstruction die. The history explicitly shows that fixed-gauge square classes are unreliable and must be tested against the full affine/lower-plus-high correction kernel.The t-direction is different. OverAt=R/(2,t2),the row becomes(s2+1,¬†s‚ąí1)=(0,t).Here there is no Frobenius square ghost at the first step. The t-direction is derivational: it measures the failure of the s-shift to be trivial to first order. So the analogous defect is notő©+U[2],but a derivation compatibility condition: the t-coefficient tail must be absorbable by the ordinary correction boundary plus the infinitesimal s-derivation of the already chosen residual correction data. In schematic form, the test isőīt (V,ő©)‚ąąimőļm(t),where őļm(t)is the map from the kernel of the lower t-ghost to the next obstruction quotient. The t-case is therefore closer to ordinary deformation theory than the 2-case, but it still cannot be reduced to a fixed tangent map, because the lower residual base may have to be retuned.So the coefficient problem has now narrowed to a concrete uniform criterion:

For¬†every¬†m,¬†őļm(2)and¬†őļm(t)must¬†kill¬†the¬†actual¬†2-¬†and¬†t-ghost¬†defects.

A stronger sufficient condition would beőļm(2)surjective¬†onto¬†Lm[2]andőļm(t)surjective¬†onto¬†the¬†relevant¬†t-defect¬†quotient for all m‚Č•5. If this holds, the coefficient nonemptiness lemma follows by induction on the m=(2,t)-adic order. Then Turn 21‚Äôs corrected compactness system gives the desired inverse-limit over-G automorphism, hence őďS‚ČÖGőďH. The compactness framework itself is already set up using coefficient-and-degree truncations P/PN,J, not the invalid degree-only quotients.This turn therefore does not disprove the isomorphism. It also does not complete the proof. It removes a misleading proof route and replaces it with a sharper finite coefficient test.

The important structural lesson is:

Do¬†not¬†test¬†A2 by¬†asking¬†whether¬†every¬†group-like¬†error¬†has¬†a¬†Witt¬†ghost.Instead, test whether the actual Nielsen correction torsor has enough kernel-square freedom to absorb the Witt defect. That is the coefficient-level analogue of the residual torsor computations that killed degrees 10,12,14,16,18,20. The residual tower is already closed by Cm=Lm for all m‚Č•5; the missing work is to prove the same kind of kernel-defect surjectivity for the coefficient ghosts.

ESTABLISHED: Turn 24‚Äôs ghost formulation is the right direction, but group-likeness alone does not prove ghost compatibility. Over A2=R/(4,t), a group-like term 1+2W need not carry the square ghost W[2]. Therefore the coefficient lift must be formulated through actual Nielsen-torsor defect maps őļm(2)and őļm(t), not through a bare Hopf-algebra group-likeness argument.

GAP: The finite coefficient nonemptiness lemma remains unproved. The exact missing statement is that the actual 2-ghost defects and t-ghost defects arising from the relator equation lie in the images of the lower-plus-high kernel maps őļm(2)and őļm(t)for every root degree and every finite coefficient quotient.

NEXT: Compute the first nontrivial defect maps őļm(2)and őļm(t)over R/(4,t) and R/(2,t2), beginning with m=5,6,7,8. If they are surjective, promote the pattern to an m-adic induction; if one has deficient rank, test the actual defect class against its cokernel.[TURN25-GHOST-COMPATIBILITY-NOT-FROM-GROUPLIKENESS]
